title: How Well Do You Know the Bible?
description: Ten questions covering biblical structure, genre, canon, gospel, interpretation and Christian hope.

questions.question: Which description best reflects what the Bible is?
options: A single book written by one human author
A library of writings produced across different periods, genres and settings
A collection of moral sayings arranged by topic
A history of Christianity beginning with Jesus
questions.explanation: The Bible is a collection of writings produced in different historical settings and literary genres. Christians read these diverse books as a unified story of God’s work in creation, Israel, Christ and new creation.
questions.link_text: Read How We Got the Bible

questions.question: Which Gospel begins with the words, ‘In the beginning was the Word’?
options: Matthew
Mark
Luke
John
questions.explanation: John begins by identifying Jesus as the eternal Word who was with God and was God. This opening connects Jesus with creation and prepares for John’s emphasis on his divine identity.
questions.link_text: Read What Is the Gospel?

questions.question: Why does recognising biblical genre matter?
options: It determines whether a passage is inspired
It helps us read poetry, narrative, law, letters and visions according to how they communicate
It allows difficult passages to be ignored
It proves that only historical narratives are trustworthy
questions.explanation: Genre helps us understand how a passage communicates truth. Poetry uses imagery differently from narrative, while proverbs, letters and apocalyptic visions each require appropriate reading strategies.
questions.link_text: Read How to Interpret the Bible

questions.question: What is the central announcement of the Christian gospel?
options: Good people will receive a better life
Jesus died for sins, was raised and reigns as Lord
Israel’s law has been replaced by personal spirituality
Human beings can reach God through sufficient knowledge
questions.explanation: The gospel centres on what God has done through Jesus: his life, death for sins, resurrection and lordship. It calls people to repentance, faith and participation in God’s renewed creation.
questions.link_text: Read What Is the Gospel?

questions.question: Which statement best explains the biblical canon?
options: The list of books Christians receive as Scripture
A translation method used for ancient manuscripts
The order in which every biblical book was written
A set of rules for interpreting prophecy
questions.explanation: Canon refers to the recognised collection of writings received as Scripture. Christian traditions agree on the New Testament but differ somewhat over the extent of the Old Testament canon.
questions.link_text: Explore the development of the canon

questions.question: When reading an Old Testament law, what should Christians consider first?
options: How to enforce it directly in a modern nation
Its place in Israel’s covenant, its purpose and how it relates to Christ
Whether it agrees with current cultural values
Whether it appears in the middle or at the end of a chapter
questions.explanation: Old Testament laws belonged to Israel’s covenant life. Christians should consider their historical setting and purpose, how they fit the wider biblical story and how their moral wisdom is fulfilled and reframed through Christ.
questions.link_text: Read How Should Christians Read Old Testament Law?

questions.question: What is the primary Christian hope presented at the end of the biblical story?
options: Disembodied souls escaping creation forever
The destruction of the physical universe
Resurrection and God’s renewal of creation
A return to life under the Sinai covenant
questions.explanation: The biblical story moves towards resurrection, judgement and new creation. Revelation ends with God dwelling with humanity as creation is renewed, not with creation being permanently abandoned.
questions.link_text: See the site’s current position on Christian hope

questions.question: Which statement best describes a parable of Jesus?
options: A fictional story with no connection to truth
A legal command that must be applied word for word
A story or comparison that reveals and challenges through the message of God’s kingdom
A prediction that always describes the end times
questions.explanation: Jesus’ parables use familiar scenes, stories and comparisons to reveal the kingdom, confront assumptions and call hearers to respond. They should not be flattened into allegories in which every detail has a hidden meaning.
questions.link_text: Learn more about reading biblical genres

questions.question: What does it mean to read a passage in context?
options: Reading only the verse that answers the question
Considering its surrounding passage, book, historical setting and place in the biblical story
Accepting the interpretation that is most familiar
Reading every sentence as a direct command to the modern reader
questions.explanation: Context includes the sentences around a verse, the argument or story of the book, the original audience and the passage’s place within Scripture. Context guards against using isolated verses to support conclusions they were not intended to teach.
questions.link_text: Read the guide to biblical interpretation

questions.question: According to 2 Timothy 3:16–17, what is Scripture intended to do?
options: Answer every possible scientific question
Equip God’s people for every good work
Remove the need for Christian teachers and community
Provide a secret code for predicting modern events
questions.explanation: Paul describes Scripture as God-breathed and useful for teaching, correction and training in righteousness so that God’s servant may be equipped for every good work. Its purpose is formative as well as informative.
questions.link_text: Read how Christians describe the Bible

questions.question: Which section of the Bible contains the books of Joshua, Judges, Samuel and Kings?
options: The Wisdom Literature
The Historical Books
The Major Prophets
The General Epistles
questions.explanation: These books narrate Israel’s life in the land, the period of the judges, the monarchy and the exile. Their history is also theological, showing the consequences of covenant faithfulness and unfaithfulness.
questions.link_text: Read How We Got the Bible

questions.question: Who led Israel out of slavery in Egypt?
options: Abraham
Joseph
Moses
Joshua
questions.explanation: God called Moses to confront Pharaoh and lead Israel out of Egypt. The exodus became one of the Bible’s central pictures of deliverance and redemption.
questions.link_text: Read How to Interpret the Bible

questions.question: Which covenant sign was given to Noah after the flood?
options: Circumcision
The rainbow
The Sabbath
The Passover meal
questions.explanation: The rainbow was given as the sign of God’s covenant with Noah, his descendants and every living creature.
questions.link_text: Read How to Interpret the Bible

questions.question: What is the first book of the New Testament?
options: Mark
Luke
Matthew
Acts
questions.explanation: Matthew is the first book in the New Testament’s canonical order, although many scholars consider Mark to have been written earlier.
questions.link_text: Read How We Got the Bible

questions.question: Which book tells the story of the early church after Jesus’ ascension?
options: Romans
Acts
Hebrews
Revelation
questions.explanation: Acts continues Luke’s Gospel and describes the Spirit-empowered spread of the gospel from Jerusalem towards the wider Roman world.
questions.link_text: Read What Is the Gospel?

questions.question: Which apostle wrote many of the New Testament letters?
options: Peter
John
Paul
James
questions.explanation: Paul wrote letters to churches and individuals addressing theology, discipleship, church life and pastoral concerns.
questions.link_text: Read What Does Paul Mean by ‘My Gospel’?

questions.question: What does the word ‘gospel’ most directly mean?
options: Religious law
Good news
Secret wisdom
Moral instruction
questions.explanation: The gospel is good news, especially the announcement that God’s saving reign has come through the life, death and resurrection of Jesus Christ.
questions.link_text: Read What Is the Gospel?

questions.question: Which event stands at the centre of Christian hope?
options: The rebuilding of Jerusalem
The resurrection of Jesus
The translation of Scripture
The founding of the Roman Empire
questions.explanation: Jesus’ resurrection is the decisive victory over sin and death and the beginning of the new creation Christians await in fullness.
questions.link_text: Read New Creation, Not Escape

questions.question: Which book contains many prayers, songs and laments?
options: Leviticus
Psalms
Acts
Revelation
questions.explanation: Psalms gathers prayers and songs of praise, lament, thanksgiving, wisdom and trust. Its poetic language should be read as poetry.
questions.link_text: Read How to Interpret the Bible

questions.question: Which statement best describes biblical prophecy?
options: It is only prediction of distant future events
It communicates God’s message, often calling people back to covenant faithfulness
It is always written in symbolic visions
It replaces the need to read the rest of Scripture
questions.explanation: Biblical prophets sometimes spoke about the future, but they also confronted injustice, idolatry and covenant unfaithfulness in their own time.
questions.link_text: Read How to Interpret the Bible

questions.question: Why can comparing several Bible translations be helpful?
options: It guarantees that every difficult passage becomes simple
It reveals where translators have made different legitimate choices
It proves that the original languages are unnecessary
It allows readers to select whichever wording supports their opinion
questions.explanation: Translations balance accuracy, clarity and style in different ways. Comparing them can highlight interpretive choices and prompt closer study.
questions.link_text: Read Bible Translations

questions.question: What is a covenant in the biblical story?
options: A private spiritual feeling
A binding relationship established with promises and responsibilities
A collection of unrelated laws
A prediction that cannot be changed
questions.explanation: Biblical covenants establish relationships involving God’s promises, human responsibilities and signs. They help organise the Bible’s unfolding story.
questions.link_text: Read How to Interpret the Bible

questions.question: What was the purpose of the tabernacle in Israel’s wilderness journey?
options: It was Israel’s royal palace
It represented God dwelling among his people
It stored the nation’s military weapons
It replaced the covenant
questions.explanation: The tabernacle was the portable sanctuary where God’s presence was represented among Israel and where covenant worship took place.
questions.link_text: Read How to Interpret the Bible

questions.question: Which command did Jesus identify as the greatest?
options: Build the largest possible congregation
Love God with all your being
Avoid all contact with sinners
Master every detail of biblical chronology
questions.explanation: Jesus identified wholehearted love for God as the greatest command and joined it with love for one’s neighbour.
questions.link_text: Read What Is the Gospel?

questions.question: What does repentance involve in the New Testament?
options: Feeling embarrassed without changing direction
Turning towards God with a changed mind and life
Earning forgiveness through punishment
Learning religious vocabulary
questions.explanation: Repentance is a reorientation towards God that includes changed thinking, allegiance and behaviour. It is more than regret alone.
questions.link_text: Read What Is the Gospel?

questions.question: Which statement best describes the kingdom of God in Jesus’ teaching?
options: Only a place people enter after death
God’s saving reign breaking into the world through Jesus
A political territory governed by the church
A synonym for the Roman Empire
questions.explanation: Jesus announced God’s reign as present in his ministry while also teaching believers to await its future fullness.
questions.link_text: Read What Is the Gospel?

questions.question: Why is it risky to build a doctrine from one isolated verse?
options: Individual verses are never inspired
The verse may be misunderstood apart from its literary and biblical context
Only church leaders may interpret Scripture
Doctrine should never use biblical texts
questions.explanation: Sound doctrine should arise from careful reading of passages in context and from the broader witness of Scripture, not from detached phrases.
questions.link_text: Read How to Interpret the Bible

questions.question: What is the relationship between the Old and New Testaments?
options: They tell unrelated stories
The New Testament continues and fulfils the story begun in the Old Testament
The Old Testament has no value for Christians
The New Testament simply repeats every Old Testament law
questions.explanation: The New Testament presents Jesus within Israel’s story and draws deeply on the Old Testament’s creation, covenant, promise, prophecy and hope.
questions.link_text: Read How We Got the Bible

questions.question: Which phrase best summarises the Bible’s final vision in Revelation 21–22?
options: Humanity permanently escaping creation
God dwelling with humanity in a renewed creation
The abandonment of the earth
A return to the garden without resurrection
questions.explanation: The Bible ends with heaven and earth united, creation renewed and God dwelling with resurrected humanity.
questions.link_text: Read New Creation, Not Escape

questions.question: What is a responsible response when a Bible passage is difficult or unclear?
options: Ignore the passage
Read its context, compare translations and consult reliable resources
Assume the first interpretation you hear must be correct
Choose the meaning that feels most comfortable
questions.explanation: Difficult passages call for patience, context, comparison and humility. Good interpretation welcomes careful study rather than forced certainty.
questions.link_text: Read How to Interpret the Bible
